title: The Collapse of Unentangled Stoquastic Merlin-Arthur Proof Systems
abstract: Entanglement and interference are among the most fundamental properties of quantum mechanics. In this work, we investigate the role and power of interference in the context of detecting entanglement. We do so from a computational complexity lens by proving that unentanglement gives no additional power to stoquastic Merlin-Arthur verification. For every polynomial number of provers $k=k(n)$, \[   \text{StoqMa}(k)=\text{StoqMa} . \] Conceptually, the proof separates the role of entanglement from the role of interference: once destructive interference is ruled out by stoquasticity, the product-state constraint can be absorbed into a polynomially larger one-witness stoquastic verification.   The main analytic ingredient is a positive, value-based de Finetti theorem for separately symmetric extensions. If $M$ is an entrywise nonnegative positive semidefinite contraction on $A_1\otimes\cdots\otimes A_k$, then the nonnegative product value of $M$ is approximated to additive error $ε$ by the largest eigenvalue of \[   Π_R^{<k}   (M_{A_{1,1}\cdots A_{k-1,1}A_k}\otimes I)   Π_R^{<k},   \qquad   R=O\!\left(\frac{k^2\sum_i\log\dim A_i}{ε^3}\right), \] where $Π_R^{<k}$ is the operator on $A_1^{\otimes R} \otimes \cdots \otimes A_{k-1}^{\otimes R} \otimes A_k$ projecting to the subspace $\mathrm{Sym}^R(A_1) \otimes \cdots \otimes \mathrm{Sym}^{R}(A_{k-1}) \otimes A_k$.   The spectral relaxation is then realized as an actual one-witness stoquastic verifier. After replacing the uniform permutation averages in the symmetric projectors by inverse-polynomially close dyadic inverse-invariant averages. Consequently, \[   \text{StoqMa}(k)=\text{StoqMa}\subseteq\text{AM}\cap\text{PP}\subseteq\text{PSPACE} . \] The positive de Finetti theorem is isolated as a standalone technique and may be useful in other nonnegative tensor-optimization and stoquastic-verification settings.
summary: This paper analyzes the computational power of multi-prover quantum verification protocols under the stoquastic constraint. It proves that the ability to use multiple provers ($k$) does not enhance the verification power beyond that of a single prover ($	ext{StoqMA}$). The key tool is a specialized de Finetti theorem, leading to the collapse $	ext{StoqMA}(k) = 	ext{StoqMA}$ for specific gap conditions.
main_problem: The paper investigates the role of interference in quantum computation complexity by proving that unentanglement does not increase the power of stoquastic Merlin-Arthur verification.
main_result: It establishes the collapse $	ext{StoqMA}(k) = 	ext{StoqMA}$ for polynomially bounded $k$ and inverse-polynomial promise gaps, implying $	ext{StoqMA}(k) \subseteq 	ext{AM} \cap 	ext{PP} \subseteq 	ext{PSPACE}$.
method: The core method involves applying a positive, value-based de Finetti theorem for separately symmetric extensions to relate the product state value to the largest eigenvalue of a projected operator.
model_or_system: The model is quantum computational complexity, specifically comparing $k$-prover stoquastic Merlin-Arthur verification ($	ext{StoqMA}(k)$) against one-witness verification ($	ext{StoqMA}$). The constraints involve stoquasticity (nonnegative, positive semidefinite contraction).
key_observables: Acceptance probability, Hellinger distance, Relative Entropy, and the non-negative product value $\omega^+(M)$.
important_parameters: The number of provers $k$, the promise gap $\Delta(n)$ (required to be $\ge 1/	ext{poly}(n)$), and the approximation error $\epsilon$.
main_assumptions: The result is contingent on the test matrix $M$ being entrywise nonnegative and the promise gap being inverse-polynomial. The de Finetti theorem used is a one-sided, test-dependent value theorem.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates the complexity landscape, confirming the equality $	ext{StoqMA} = 	ext{StoqMA}(2) = 	ext{StoqQMA}(2) = 	ext{StoqMA}(k)$ under the specified gap conditions.
paper_structure: The paper builds from establishing the positive de Finetti theorem for separately symmetric extensions (Chunk 3), to realizing this spectral relaxation as an actual one-witness verifier (Chunk 2), and finally applying this construction to prove the collapse $	ext{StoqMA}(k) = 	ext{StoqMA}$ (Chunk 4).
why_interesting: This work provides a deep structural result in quantum complexity theory, showing that for certain restricted quantum protocols (stoquastic), the ability to use multiple entangled provers offers no computational advantage over a single prover.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Complexity landscape around the result. The highlighted equality is proved here for inverse- polynomial promise gaps by the direct k-prover collapse in Theorem 5.1. The dashed arrow is the direct BKS sum-of-squares simulation for nonnegative forms [22], which gives an EXP upper bound in the exponentially large witness dimension and is superseded, for stoquastic verifiers, by the collapse. The containment StoqMA ⊆AM follows from the stoquastic largest-eigenvalue/approximate-set-size framework of Bravyi–DiVincenzo–Oliveira–Terhal together with the original stoquastic-verification framework [13, 14]; StoqMA ⊆PP follows from StoqMA ⊆QMA ⊆PP [35].
